nativeRange.habitat: Calm, shallow freshwater: swamps, marshes, floodplains, rivers, and lake margins with fine substrates; inhabits areas with low dissolved oxygen where access to surface air is critical

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential — abrasive gravel damages belly)
smooth water-worn rocks and spindly driftwood arranged into caves
dense planting with tall, robust species (Vallisneria, large Anubias, Aponogeton)
dim or diffused lighting (shade-tolerant cover plants help)
gentle filtration output — strong currents are avoided in the wild
tight-fitting lid with all gaps sealed (foam/mesh) to prevent escape

diet.notes: Strictly carnivorous; hunts primarily by smell (vision is poor). Feed live or frozen meaty foods: earthworms, prawns, mussels, krill, bloodworms, chopped fish fillet, and lancefish. Smaller specimens accept brine shrimp and bloodworms; larger individuals need chunkier prey. Some specimens can be trained to accept high-quality sinking carnivore pellets once settled. Feeds 2–3 times per week in maturity. Messy eater — remove uneaten food promptly to prevent ammonia spikes; strong biological filtration is essential.

goodWith: other large Polypterus species of similar size
large African cichlids (Hemichromis, Haplochromis, Tilapia, Severum-type)
medium-to-large characins too big to swallow
avoidWith: any fish small enough to fit in its mouth (will be eaten overnight)
fin-nipping species (rasboras, tiger barbs, serpae tetras)
aggressive cichlids that target the bichir's soft belly
Senegal Bichir (P. senegalus) unless tank is very large — size mismatch risk

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: Citron. Clear lateral view of adult P. ornatipinnis showing bold black-and-cream banded pattern; also shows P. senegalus in same frame for size context. High resolution.

summary: The Ornate Bichir is the largest of the 'upper-jaw' Polypterus group, reaching 60 cm with a striking bold cream-and-black banded pattern. A living fossil from an ancient Devonian-era lineage, it is an obligate air-breather, highly escape-prone, and a nocturnal carnivore requiring a very large, tightly covered tank.
careNotes: Polypterus ornatipinnis belongs to family Polypteridae — one of the oldest ray-finned fish lineages, with relatives dating to the Devonian (fossils of the broader actinopterygian stem group ~420 Ma). The species retains paired, lung-like swim bladders with which it breathes atmospheric air; it is an obligate air-breather and MUST have access to the water surface at all times — a sealed lid with no air gap will cause drowning. The tank lid must be fully secured: gaps where cables or tubing enter should be plugged with fine mesh or filter foam, as bichirs can squeeze through surprisingly small openings and survive considerable time out of water. Tank size: a minimum 650 L (approximately 180×60×60 cm) tank is required for a single adult; additional Polypterus species will each add significant bioload. Use fine sand substrate exclusively — gravel or coarse substrate abrades the belly and causes chronic ulceration. Lighting should be low or heavily diffused; the species is strictly nocturnal and spends daylight hours sheltering in caves. Feed after lights-out for best feeding response. Water quality: this species produces substantial waste from its high-protein diet, making efficient biological filtration non-negotiable. Nitrates should be kept below 20 ppm; ammonia spikes after feeding are a real risk — siphon uneaten food within an hour of feeding. Despite its prehistoric appearance, the Ornate Bichir is relatively peaceful toward tankmates it cannot swallow; conspecific and same-genus tankmates are usually tolerated if size-matched. Its copper sensitivity makes it incompatible with copper-based treatments applied to the main display. See also: Senegal Bichir (P. senegalus, fw-fish-127) — smaller, more beginner-friendly congener.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquaria; most specimens are wild-caught. Sexual maturity is reached at approximately 5–6 years. Spawning is triggered by simulating rainy-season conditions: lowering temperature slightly, reducing pH toward 6.5, and performing large water changes with slightly cooler water. During spawning the male cups his anal fin around the female's genital region and fertilises eggs externally; eggs are adhesive and scatter among fine-leafed plants. Up to 300 eggs per spawn; parents will consume eggs and should be removed after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days at 26°C; larvae bear external gills (reabsorbed within weeks) and become free-swimming after absorbing the yolk sac. Fry are fed baby brine shrimp and micro-worms; juveniles grow quickly and must be size-sorted to prevent predation.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Polypterus ornatapinnis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size 60 cm, temperature 77–82°F (25–28°C), pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 5–25°H, minimum tank 650 L (180×60×60 cm), native range (Cameroon, DRC, Angola, Burundi, Tanzania), habitat, diet (live/frozen meaty foods; dislikes dried), compatible species list, nocturnal/escape-risk behaviour, and breeding trigger notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Polypterus ornatipinnis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (60 cm TL), temperature 26–28°C, native range (Congo basin, Lake Tanganyika, Lake Rukwa drainage), taxonomy (Polypteridae, Polypteriformes, class Cladistii), diet (worms and insect larvae when young; mainly fish when adult), demersal freshwater habitat, trophic level 3.7, dorsal spines 9–11.

additional.name: Aquariadise — Ornate Bichir
additional.notes: Confirmed water parameters (pH 6.9–8.0, hardness up to 25 dH, temperature 77–82°F), minimum tank 540 L (6×2×2 ft), captive adult size ~58 cm, breeding behaviour (male anal-fin embrace, adhesive eggs, 3–4 day hatch), and feeding details (frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, earthworms, prawns, mussels, krill; sinking pellets accepted when soaked).

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Ornate Bichir
additional.notes: Supplementary trade-care guide. Confirmed lifespan 10–20 years, copper sensitivity, escape risk (primitive lungs allow extended land survival), obligate air-breathing note, sexual maturity at 5–6 years, and up to 300 eggs per spawn. Minimum tank listed as 180 gallons (~680 L).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Polypterus ornatipinnis
additional.notes: Confirmed 9–11 dorsal spines, black-and-yellow patterning, 60 cm maximum length, Lake Tanganyika and Congo River basin range, obligate air-breather (divided swim bladder), and living fossil context (Polypteridae relatives dating to ancient actinopterygian stem lineage).

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity; most specimens are wild-caught. Spawning triggered by rainy-season simulation (temperature drop, pH drop toward 6.5, large cool water changes). Male cups anal fin to fertilise eggs externally; up to 300 adhesive eggs scattered among plants. Hatch in 3–4 days; larvae bear reabsorbing external gills. Remove parents after spawning to prevent egg predation.
